title: Measuring the Quality of Mergers and Acquisitions
abstract: We develop a measure of merger and acquisition (M&A) quality using accounting theory. This measure, implied return-on-equity improvement (IRI), quantifies the minimum improvement in the target’s post-acquisition return on equity (ROE) the acquirer must attain to break even on the acquisition price. Employing a large sample of M&As from 1980 to 2018, we find that a high IRI is, on average, less attainable ex post and predicts worse acquirer financial performance. The acquirer’s ROE growth over the first three years after the M&A is 11 percentage points lower for high-IRI M&As compared with low-IRI M&As. Worse high-IRI acquirer performance is observable through higher operating costs, tighter financial constraints, lower investments, and larger and more frequent goodwill impairments. We also find that IRI increases with acquiring chief executive officers’ overconfidence, incentive misalignment, and difficulty in estimating synergies; IRI decreases with acquirers’ financial discipline and due diligence effort. As such, overestimating synergies and managerial incentives that drive overpayment are potential mechanisms underlying IRI’s negative association with acquirers’ post-M&A performance.

author.Atif Ellahie: David Eccles School of Business, University of Utah, Salt Lake City, Utah 84112;
author.Shenje Hshieh: College of Business, City University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong;
author.Feng Zhang: Cox School of Business, Southern Methodist University, Dallas, Texas 75275

article.1. Introduction: Mergers and acquisitions (M&As) are among the most prominent types of firm investments. Between 1980 and 2018, U.S. public firms invested an average of $92 million per year on M&As, constituting 18% of annual investments and 27% of annual profits over the same period.1Considering these values, assessing the quality of an M&A deal before undertaking it continues to hold both practical and academic importance. In this study, we construct a measure of M&A deal quality grounded in established accounting and finance principles; this measure is easy to compute and can assist acquirers in assessing the quality of a contemplated M&A deal before announcing it. Constructing a quality measure that captures the multiple dimensions of M&A deal quality is no small task. Although most acquirers justify M&As with financial synergies, some acquirers may buy target firms to boost growth, strengthen their market power, or access targets’ talent and intellectual property (Berkovitch and Narayanan 1993,Chen et al. 2024). Yet, unless an M&A enhances the acquirer’s financial performance, the deal is arguably of poor quality. We, therefore, focus on M&As’ financial quality because financial synergies are a principal theoretical motivation for decisions on the firm boundary (e.g.,Lewellen 1971,Grossman and Hart 1986,Leland 2007). Furthermore, the acquirer’s preclosing due diligence centers on expected financial benefits. Our proposed measure, which we refer to as the implied return on equity improvement (IRI), combines prime characteristics of an acquirer-target pair into an index measure of the M&A’s financial quality. IRI has broad applicability across different strategic rationales for M&A. Building on the clean surplus accounting identity (Feltham and Ohlson 1995;Ohlson 1995;Vuolteenaho 2000,2002), we model and estimate the minimum post-acquisition improvement in the target firm’s return on equity (ROE) that is required for the acquirer to break even on the offer price. A high-quality M&A is one in which the target’s IRI (i.e., its post-M&A ROE improvement implied by the deal terms) is attainable ex post and thus, enhances the acquirer’s ROE. IRI is conceptually analogous to the internal rate of return (IRR). An investment project adds value to the firm if IRR exceeds the firm’s cost of capital; an M&A benefits the acquirer if IRI is below the target’s true, attainable post-M&A ROE improvement. We test whether IRI measured at the time of deal announcement predicts the acquirer’s post-M&A ROE growth within a framework conceptually built on synergy estimation and overpayment. If an acquirer correctly estimates synergies and does not overpay, IRI should capture the target’s true, attainable post-M&A ROE improvement. Under thissynergy hypothesis, IRI positively predicts the acquirer’s post-M&A ROE growth. If an acquirer overestimates synergies or overpays, IRI will exceed the target’s true, attainable post-M&A ROE improvement. Under thisoverpayment hypothesis, IRI negatively predicts the acquirer’s post-M&A ROE growth. The sign of the association between IRI and the acquirer’s post-M&A ROE growth depends on which hypothesis dominates in the cross-section of M&As. Of course, it is also possible that IRI is not significantly related to the acquirer’s post-M&A ROE growth. We use a large sample of M&As from 1980 to 2018 to empirically examine whether IRI performs as theory would predict. We find that acquirers undertaking high-IRI M&As (high-IRI acquirers hereafter) perform worse after the M&A.2The ROE of high-IRI acquirers (i.e., those with IRIs in the top quartile) declines by 11 percentage points more than that of low-IRI acquirers over the first three years after the M&A. Based on the average book equity of $1.6 billion in the sample, high-IRI acquirers underperform low-IRI acquirers by $176 million ($1.6 billion × 11%) per year in terms of earnings. This result is consistent with the overpayment hypothesis and suggests that IRI is an inverse measure of M&A quality. Furthermore, IRI’s predictive power for post-M&A acquirer performance remains robust using (1) alternative measures of operating performance, including residual earnings return on equity, operating return on equity, and net operating margin; (2) alternative model specifications; (3) a longer performance window of five years; (4) a sample of larger deals in which the acquisition has greater impact on the acquirer’s performance; and (5) alternative performance measures based on stock and bond returns. IRI’s predictive power is also incremental to an extensive list of 19 firm and deal characteristics examined in prior studies, including acquirer-target combined stock returns around the M&A announcement; earnings per share (EPS) accretion or dilution; proxies for the strength of the acquirer’s corporate governance; and the similarity and complementarity between the acquirer’s and the target’s products, technology, and human capital among others.3These 19 characteristics are based on disparate aspects of the M&A, whereas IRI relies on accounting theory and combines prime deal and firm characteristics into an index measure of financial quality. IRI’s incremental predictive power is thus unsurprising and further demonstrates that IRI is a consistent measure of deal quality that inversely predicts various measures of post-M&A acquirer performance. Our conceptual framework and empirical results suggest that overestimated synergies and overpayment drive IRI’s predictive power for post-M&A acquirer performance. Additional tests of IRI’s underlying economic mechanisms show that IRI is higher when estimating synergies is difficult or when the acquiring chief executive officer (CEO) is overconfident and weakly incentivized. Conversely, IRI is lower when the acquirer is more financially disciplined and expends greater due diligence effort. On balance, these results suggest that CEO overconfidence, misaligned incentives, and difficulty in estimating synergies could explain why high-IRI acquirers overestimate synergies or overpay. Lastly, we find that high-IRI acquirers incur higher operating and unforeseen costs after the M&A, suggesting that these acquirers have likely overestimated synergies. High-IRI acquirers also experience lower liquidity, greater financial constraints, and foregone investments, probably because having overpaid for the target, they experience liquidity issues and fewer resources for investment.4Further, we find that high-IRI acquirers take more frequent and larger write downs of acquisition goodwill, which we interpret as these acquirers acknowledging that they have overpaid for synergies that eventually prove to be unattainable. Our study contributes to several strands of literature. First, we add to prior studies on the role of accounting information in facilitating efficient firm investments (Biddle et al. 2009) and M&As (Raman et al. 2013,Skaife and Wangerin 2013,McNichols and Stubben 2015). Although these studies focus on the role of financial reporting quality, we develop a fundamentals-based measure of M&A quality. Our work also complements studies on M&A valuations (Eaton et al. 2022,Imperatore et al. 2024,Shaffer 2024), overinvestment (Richardson 2006), and overbidding (de Bodt et al. 2018,Bartov et al. 2021). Second, IRI adds a novel, theoretically motivated, and simple-to-compute measure of deal quality to the voluminous M&A literature (seeBetton et al. 2008for a robust literature review). In our view, IRI is an ex ante quality measure that can provide guidance to acquiring managers and advisors during the entire M&A process. Acquirers can compute an IRI for selected targets using a contemplated price and data available prior to deal announcement. Hence, internal analysis of IRI can help acquirers assess the performance improvement threshold from an M&A in the context of synergy attainability. Similarly, investors can estimate an IRI for a rumored or hypothetical M&A using an assumed range of offer prices. Once the deal is announced, the IRI estimate based on the actual offer price can help investors form their trading strategy. Third, IRI’s predictive power for post-M&A acquirer performance is incremental to the deal and firm characteristics that have been used in prior studies. IRI thus adds to the literature on M&A quality and the determinants of acquirer performance.5We also find that estimated EPS accretion/dilution and M&A announcement returns, despite their popularity, cannot predict post-M&A acquirer performance.6Lastly, by studying IRI’s underlying mechanisms, we shed light on the motives behind M&As, including findings from recent studies showing that less due diligence leads to worse acquirer performance (e.g.,Wangerin 2019).7 Because we are introducing a new method to measure M&A quality, we follow the proposal ofNoh et al. (2021)that a new method should satisfy three criteria: (1) justify the method from first principles, (2) empirically validate that the method performs as theory would predict, and (3) illustrate the importance of the method for inferences. We organize our study based on these criteria. InSection 2, we construct IRI based on established principles in accounting and finance.Section 3describes our data sample.Section 4shows that IRI behaves as theory predicts and has consistent, robust predictive power for post-M&A acquirer performance.Section 5examines the economic mechanisms that could explain why high-IRI acquirers underperform low-IRI acquirers.Section 6shows that IRI negatively predicts the acquirer’s stock and bond returns.Section 7concludes.
article.2. IRI: 2.1. Modeling IRI We model IRI based on the clean surplus accounting identity, which expresses market value of equity (MV) as a function of earnings, book value of equity (BV), and expected returns (Feltham and Ohlson 1995,Ohlson 1995). The identity is well known in the accounting literature and has also been used in finance studies. For example,Vuolteenaho (2000,2002) adopts the clean surplus identity to model the market-to-book ratio as a function of accounting returns and stock returns. In an analogous spirit, we modelMVas a function ofBVandROEgenerated overnyears post-acquisition and discounted at the cost of equity (COE): Equation (1)can be rearranged to show how market and book values of equity evolve overnyears: Although market value of equity grows at the rate of the cost of equity, book value of equity grows at the rate of return on equity. In the long run (e.g., when the firm is liquidated), the ratio of market value of equity to book value of equity is assumed to converge to a constantk; the convergence is a common feature of models based on the clean surplus accounting identity.8 We then rearrangeEquation (2)in terms of return on equity: Equation (3)says that given a COE, one can compute the ROE that isimpliedby the observed level of the market-to-book ratio. Parallels with the implied cost of capital (ICC) literature are obvious. Although the ICC literature solves for COE implied by market price and forecasts of future ROE, our approach solves for future ROE implied by the market-to-book ratio and COE.9 We applyEquation (3)to the M&A setting to compute the implied post-M&A ROE of the target firm: 𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝐼𝑚𝑝R⁢O⁢EImpis the minimum post-M&A ROE of the target firm that the acquirer must generate to break even on the deal. The difference between𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝐼𝑚𝑝R⁢O⁢EImpand the target’s ROE before acquisition (i.e.,𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝑃𝑟𝑒R⁢O⁢EPre) quantifies the minimum improvement in the target’s ROE that the acquirer must generate over the investment horizon to justify the acquisition price. We call this the IRI: IRI combines six prime deal/firm characteristics—the investment horizon, the target’s market-to-book ratio, the weighted-average cost of equity of the acquirer and target, the target’s profitability before M&A, and the level of the bid premium—into an index measure of the M&A’s financial quality. 2.2. Computing IRI and a Case Study of Six Recent M&As Among IRI’s six inputs, five can be computed using data available before the M&A announcement. We use a five-year investment horizon (i.e.,n= 5) in the baseline results and show that the results are robust to alternative horizons. A five-year horizon is consistent with the median CEO tenure of five years and the average tenure of six years since the 1980s (Graham et al. 2020). Without loss of generality, we setkequal to one. Doing so simplifies the computation without qualitatively affecting our empirical analysis. We compute the target’s market-to-book ratio and ROEprior tothe M&A announcement using its latest annual financial data from Compustat. We proxy for the COE inEquation (4)with the acquirer’s and the target’s weighted-average COEprior tothe M&A announcement. The sixth input, bid premium, becomes public information once the M&A is announced. Because acquirer managers know the bid premium (or a hypothetical bid premium) when negotiating with the target firm, they can estimate the IRI before announcing the M&A to guide their internal analysis of required synergies and deal quality. Similarly, investors can estimate the IRI of a rumored M&A using an assumed range of prices, and once the M&A is announced, they can re-estimate the IRI using the actual price. Thus, IRI has the potential to guide investors in their investment decisions involving the acquirer. Although IRI inputs are only available to public investors if the acquirer and the target are both public firms, public and private acquirer managers can estimate IRI before the M&A announcement even if the target is a private firm. COE can be estimated using stock returns of public firms comparable with the acquirer and the target before deal announcement; acquirer managers know the contemplated acquisition price and likely have confidential access to the financial data of the private target (including book value of equity and pre-M&A ROE) during the negotiation process. The COE inEquation (4)should reflect risk appropriate for the post-acquisition improvement in earnings, which should depend on the risk inherent in the assets required to generate those earnings. As econometricians, we cannot observe which assets are used to improve ROE. Following common practice in corporate development departments and in the M&A advisory industry, we use the acquirer’s and the target’s weighted-average COE before the deal announcement, where the weights are based on the pre-M&A market values of equity (see Table A.1 in the Online Appendix for details). We use the weighted-average pre-M&A COE because the assets of the acquirer-target merged entity are likely utilized to improve earnings. In robustness tests, we find that our baseline results remain qualitatively unchanged if we compute IRI using the target’s pre-M&A COE, the acquirer’s pre-M&A COE, or a constant 10% COE. To illustrate, we calculate the IRI for Albemarle’s acquisition of Rockwood announced on July 15, 2014 in Table A.1 in the Online Appendix. As discussed,nis set to five, andkis set to one. Albemarle paid Rockwood’s shareholders a bid premium of 11.9%. Rockwood’s MV/BV was 1.9, and its ROE was 2.5% before the acquisition announcement. The weighted-average pre-M&A COE is 15.0% based on the acquirer’s and the target’s Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM-based) COE estimates using their stock returns before the announcement. Plugging these input values inEquations (4)and (5) yields an IRI estimate of 31.3%. This relatively high IRI indicates that Albemarle needs to improve Rockwood’s ROE from 2.5% before the acquisition to 33.8% to justify the price paid for Rockwood. However, improving Rockwood’s ROE by 31.3% turns out to be too challenging; Albemarle’s ROE declines by an average of 5.3% over the three years after the acquisition. We present IRI inputs and IRI estimates for an additional five M&As in Table A.1 in the Online Appendix. Across these deals, we observe worse post-acquisition acquirer performance when the IRI is high. High-IRI acquirers are also more likely to write down their goodwill. The CECO–PFMG acquisition and the Dentsply–Sirona merger, which have higher IRIs of 48.8% and 34.7%, respectively, witness considerable goodwill impairments over the three years after the deal. In contrast, acquisitions with lower IRIs, such as the Rockville–United acquisition (9.4%) and the Geo–Cornell acquisition (6.9%), are less challenging for the acquirers (Rockville and Geo Group) to deliver, and both experience improved ROE over the three years after acquisition. Additionally, the acquirers with lower IRIs do not write down any goodwill over the three years after the M&A. Taken together, these examples illustrate that IRI is a promising measure of M&A deal quality. 2.3. How IRI Changes with the Inputs To further demonstrate that IRI’s construction is consistent with theory, we next illustrate how IRI changes with the inputs using sensitivity analysis around base case values of the inputs: a target MV/BV of 1.5, bid premium of 30%, weighted-average pre-M&A COE of 12%, horizon of five years, and target pre-acquisition ROE of 9%. These base case values, which are close to the average or median values in our sample, result in an implied ROE (𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝐼𝑚𝑝R⁢O⁢EImp) of 28.0% and an IRI of 19.0%. Figure A.1 in the Online Appendix plots how IRI varies with each input and shows that IRI increases nonlinearly with target MV/BV and bid premium and linearly with the weighted-average pre-M&A COE. IRI decreases nonlinearly with investment horizon and linearly with target ROE. Additionally, the calculated IRI for a particular M&A deal is specific to the acquirer-target pair. For example, an acquirer with a higher stand-alone COE, which would likely lead to a higher weighted-average pre-M&A COE, needs a higher implied post-M&A target ROE (i.e.,𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝐼𝑚𝑝R⁢O⁢EImp) to break even on the acquisition price, ceteris paribus. This last point is consistent with standard capital budgeting theory where an investment only adds value if its return (e.g., IRR) exceeds the cost of capital. The bid premium reflects the combined effects of both positive and negative elements of the M&A. On the positive side, the bid premium could capture the synergies of the M&A. On the negative side, it could capture the acquirer’s overestimation of synergies, a high degree of competition for the target, or lower bargaining power in negotiating favorable deal terms, all of which could lead to overpayment. Relative to the base case assumption of a bid premium of 30%, if we reduce the bid premium to 0%, the IRI declines from 19.00% to 12.46%. Similarly, a target’s market-to-book ratio above one reflects the combined effects of both positive and negative elements of firm valuation. On the positive side, a high market-to-book ratio could reflect the effects of accounting conservatism (e.g., unrecorded internally generated intangibles, such as research and development, goodwill, brand, etc.) or future growth opportunities included in market values but not in current assets in place. On the negative side, a high market-to-book ratio could reflect overvaluation by the target’s current investors. Relative to the base case assumption of a market-to-book ratio of 1.50, if we reduce the ratio to 1.0, the IRI declines from 19.00% to 9.03%. We argue that an IRI driven by more positive elements of the M&A deal is of higher quality than one that is driven by more negative elements. If the target’s market-to-book ratio and the bid premium are composed primarily of negative elements or if the acquirer and target have high COEs, then𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝐼𝑚𝑝R⁢O⁢EImpmay be too high for the acquirer to achieve. Conversely, if the target’s market-to-book ratio and the bid premium are composed primarily of positive elements or if the acquirer and target have low COEs, then𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝐼𝑚𝑝R⁢O⁢EImpmay be easier for the acquirer to achieve. Finally, the target’s ROE before acquisition (𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝑃𝑟𝑒R⁢O⁢EPre) could either increase (i.e., if loss making) or decrease (i.e., if profitable) the threshold rate of return required for the acquirer to break even on the acquisition price (i.e., by altering IRI). We conclude that the acquirer-target matching has relevant implications for (1) whether IRI reflects more positive elements (i.e., expected synergies) or more negative elements (i.e., overestimation of synergies or overpayment) and (2) the association between IRI and the acquirer’s future ROE growth. 2.4. Framework for the Relation Between IRI and the Acquirer’s Future ROE Growth According to the discussion, IRI could capture both expected synergies and overpayment implied by M&A terms. Because the true synergies are unobservable ex ante, IRI could also reflect overestimated synergies. Thus, IRI could reflect three non-mutually exclusive components: (1) implied expected synergies, (2) implied overpayment, and (3) implied overestimation of synergies. We argue that the relative contribution of these three components has implications for the sign of the association between IRI and the acquirer’s future ROE growth. We develop a conceptual framework to illuminate this point. Denote𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸*R⁢O⁢E*as the target’s true (but unobservable ex ante) post-M&A ROE, and consider two scenarios. In scenario 1, the acquirer overestimates the synergies or overpays for the target (i.e.,𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝐼𝑚𝑝>𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸*R⁢O⁢EImp>R⁢O⁢E*). Then, IRI consists of two parts: The second term (𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸*−𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝑃𝑟𝑒R⁢O⁢E*−R⁢O⁢EPre) represents the actual synergies, which are the attainable improvements in the target’s ROE. The first term (𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝐼𝑚𝑝−𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸*R⁢O⁢EImp−R⁢O⁢E*) is unattainable and reflects overestimated synergies or overpayment. If the acquirer overestimates synergies or overpays for the target, IRI could be driven primarily by the first term (𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝐼𝑚𝑝−𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸*R⁢O⁢EImp−R⁢O⁢E*). IRI could also be negatively correlated with the acquirer’s post-M&A ROE growth because a high IRI likely implies that the synergies are less attainable. In scenario 2, the acquirer does not overestimate synergies or overpay (i.e.,𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝐼𝑚𝑝≤𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸*R⁢O⁢EImp≤R⁢O⁢E*), and IRI only captures the attainable improvement in the target’s ROE. Because𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝐼𝑚𝑝≤𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸*R⁢O⁢EImp≤R⁢O⁢E*, the acquirer has some cushion between the true synergies and the break-even ROE, which increases the likelihood of attaining the synergies. Thus, in this scenario, the IRI is likely to be positively correlated with the acquirer’s post-acquisition ROE growth, which incorporates the target’s post-M&A earnings. In sum, IRI could negatively predict the acquirer’s ROE growth if the target’s post-M&A ROE implied by the deal terms (𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸𝐼𝑚𝑝R⁢O⁢EImp) exceeds the true, attainable post-M&A ROE (𝑅⁢𝑂⁢𝐸*R⁢O⁢E*) for most M&As in the sample. If not, IRI could positively predict the acquirer’s ROE growth.
article.3. Data and Summary Statistics: Our sample starts with all mergers (deal form “M”) and acquisitions of majority interest (“AM”) between U.S. public firms in the Securities Data Company (SDC) Platinum database over the 1980–2018 period. To be included in our sample, the transaction value must be greater than $10 million, the relative size (i.e., the ratio of the transaction value to the market value of the acquirer’s assets) must be higher than 10%, and the M&A must be a control bid in which the acquirer owns less than 50% of the target prior to the bid and intends to own over 50% of the target after the acquisition. These criteria are similar to those used in prior studies of post-acquisition acquirer performance (Betton et al. 2008,Bessembinder and Zhang 2013) and exclude minor M&As that are expected to have limited impact on the acquirer’s performance. We then match the acquirer and the target to firms in Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) and Compustat and exclude deals with missing deal characteristics or missing IRI input data. Our final sample has 2,352 M&As. The median IRI is 27.1% compared with the median target firm’s pre-M&A ROE of 9.4% (Table 1). The mean IRI is higher (37.8%), suggesting positively skewed IRI. To account for skewness, potential nonlinear effects of IRI, and potential measurement errors, we create a high IRI indicator for IRIs in the highest quartile across all M&As. The average M&A has a transaction value of $2.9 billion, a relative size of 49%, and a bid premium of 33%. Among the M&As, 22% are all-cash deals, 37% are all-stock deals, 34% are diversifying deals, and 76% are completed. The target firms have an average ROE of 1.24% and an average book-to-market ratio of 0.64 in the year prior to the M&A announcement. The average acquirer-target weighted-average COE is 13.4% before the M&A announcement. Over the three days (−1, +1) around the deal announcement, the weighted-average cumulative abnormal returns (CARs) to acquirer and target shareholders (i.e., the combined CARs) are 3.0%. The deal characteristics in our sample are in line with those in prior studies (Moeller et al. 2005,Betton et al. 2008). The sample includes all mergers and acquisitions of majority interest with relative size greater than 10% and transaction value greater than $10 million. All continuous variables are winsorized at the 1st and 99th percentiles. See the appendix for variable definitions. p, percentile. Figure 1plots the annual number of M&As and the annual average IRI over our sample period. It shows that the average IRI is higher in the years with more M&A activity. This is unsurprising because IRI increases with the target’s market-to-book ratio and M&A activity rises with stock market valuations (e.g.,Rhodes-Kropf et al. 2005). IRI also varies across industries, suggesting the need to control for acquirer and target industries in the regressions (see Table A.3, panel A in the Online Appendix). To understand the relation between IRI and deal characteristics, we sort the sample of M&As into quartiles based on IRI (see Table A.3, panel B in the Online Appendix) and regress IRI on the inputs to IRI and the deal characteristics (see Table A.3, panel C in the Online Appendix). We observe that IRI is positively associated with the bid premium, the acquirer-target weighted-average pre-M&A COE, and the all-stock deal indicator; IRI is negatively associated with the target’s book-to-market ratio, the transaction value, and the diversifying deal indicator. However, the IRI inputs and the deal characteristics combined explain less than one third of the variation in IRI (Table A.3, panel C in the Online Appendix), suggesting that IRI provides information incremental to these variables.
article.4. IRI and the Acquirer’s Financial Perf: In this section, we show that IRI negatively predicts the acquirer’s operating performance after the M&A. This result is robust to alternative measures of operating performance and various model specifications and remains strong after controlling for 19 deal and firm characteristics that have been used in prior studies to explain post-M&A acquirer performance. 4.1. Baseline Results We study whether IRI predicts changes in the acquirer’s ROE over the three years after an M&A.10For each of the 1,782 completed M&As, we compute the change in the acquirer’s ROE in each of the first three years after the M&A relative to the latest fiscal year before the M&A. The summary statistics inTable 2, panel A show that the average ROE drops by 4.34 percentage points in the first year, 15.63 percentage points in the second year, and 10.58 percentage points in the third year. The median ROE change follows a similar pattern, declining by 1.34, 2.53, and 2.95 percentage points in each of the first three years, respectively. Figure 2(a)plots the mean ROE changes in the first three years after the deal separately for M&As with high versus low IRIs. Whereas ROE also declines after low-IRI M&As (i.e., M&As with IRI in the bottom three quartiles), the decline is much larger for high-IRI M&As in the top quartile. This pattern holds for each of the three years post-M&A. For example, the median ROE drops by 2.3 percentage points in the third year for low-IRI M&As, which is better than the 6.4-percentage-point drop for those with high IRI. We observe comparable patterns across high- and low-IRI M&As inFigure 2(b)using an alternative measure of profitability (Net operating margin) that removes the effects of M&A financing. The patterns suggest that high-IRI acquirers have poorer operating performance after M&A. The coefficient on IRI is negative and statistically significant at the 1% level in both columns. First, the coefficient on relative size (i.e., the ratio of the transaction value to the acquirer’s market value of assets) is negative and statistically significant in columns (2) and (4) inTable 2, panel B. Worsening performance after all-stock deals is consistent with acquirers’ timing M&A when they perform better, yet the same observation after all-cash deals contradicts this argument. Third, the coefficients on bid premium and acquirer-target combined announcement returns are both insignificant, consistent with recent studies (Hoberg and Phillips 2018,Ben-David et al. 2020). 4.2. Importantly, in a comprehensive horse race, we find that IRI’s predictive power remains strong after controlling for characteristics used in prior accounting and finance studies to explain post-M&A acquirer performance. IRI complements these characteristics by bringing a new measure of quality that has incremental explanatory power. Generalizability of the Baseline Results Following the literature, our empirical analysis has focused on completed M&As because targets of withdrawn deals do not affect the acquirer’s performance. That M&A completion is endogenous does not invalidate our estimates of the cross-sectional association between IRI and post-M&A performance within completed M&As. Nevertheless, curiosity invites us to assess whether IRI relates to the likelihood of deal completion and whether the baseline results for completed M&As are generalizable to all M&As (completed and withdrawn). We find that completed M&As have an average IRI of 39.7, which is higher than the average IRI of 31.9 for withdrawn M&As. The logit regression results in Table A.8, panel A in the Online Appendix also reveal that IRI is positively associated with the likelihood of deal completion. The results are consistent with the view that some deals are withdrawn because the acquirer refuses to pay a higher bid premium, which raises the IRI. Completed M&As may not be representative of the entire population of M&As. To assess the generalizability of our estimates to the population, we follow prior studies and estimate model (6) using weighted least squares regressions with weights selected to make completed M&As representative of the population on observed deal characteristics (Stuart et al. 2011,2015;Kosse et al. 2020). This method is analogous to the weighting method that adjusts for nonresponse to surveys (Little and Vartivarian 2003) and the inverse propensity of the treatment weighting method (Imbens 2004). In this method, we estimate the probability of deal completion from a logit model (Table A.8, panel A in the Online Appendix); as weights, we employ the inverse probability of completion, the overlap weight (one minus the probability of completion), or the stratification weight.16By giving larger weights to M&As with lower probabilities of completion, the objective is to make the weighted sample of completed M&As resemble the population on observable deal characteristics (Table A.8, panel B in the Online Appendix). In addition, we implement the entropy-balancing method, which uses moments of the covariate distribution to incorporate covariate balance directly into the weight function (Hainmueller 2012). Table A.8, panel C in the Online Appendix presents the weighted-regression results using these four weighting schemes. The coefficients on IRI and the high-IRI indicator have economic magnitudes similar to those in the baseline results inTable 2, panel B and remain statistically significant. Viewed together, these results suggest that the effects of IRI on post-M&A performance are likely generalizable to the whole population of M&As.
article.5. Economic Mechanisms: In this section, we show that overestimated synergies or overpayments are potential mechanisms behind IRI as an inverse measure of M&A quality.17We find that IRI is higher among acquirers with overconfident, weakly incentivized, and poorly disciplined managers; IRI also increases with the difficulty in estimating synergies. In addition, high-IRI acquirers incur higher operating and unforeseen costs after the M&A, experience reduced liquidity and more stringent financial constraints, and forego investment opportunities. High-IRI acquirers also write down more goodwill and do so more frequently. 5.1. Determinants of IRI The conceptual framework inSection 2.4suggests that the degree to which IRI captures synergies versus overpayment (or overestimation of synergies) has important implications for the sign of the relation between IRI and the acquirer’s post-M&A ROE growth. The robust negative relation between IRI and acquirer ROE growth shown inSection 4indicates that IRI primarily captures synergy overestimation or overpayment. We thus examine whether IRI is associated with acquiring and target firm characteristics that relate to synergy overestimation and overpayment. 5.1.1. Overconfidence and Incentives of Acquirer CEOs. To examine whether and how IRI varies with acquirer CEOs’ overconfidence and incentive misalignment, we constructHolder 67, a proxy for managerial overconfidence proposed byMalmendier and Tate (2005,2008) andCampbell et al. (2011). Overconfident CEOs tend to overestimate their ability to generate returns from M&As (Malmendier and Tate 2008), and CEOs are considered overconfident (Holder 67equals one) if they hold stock options that are, on average, over 67% in the money.18We find thatHolder 67is positively associated with IRI (column (1) inTable 5), which suggests that CEO overconfidence could raise the component of IRI that captures synergy overestimation or overpayment. Managers are less motivated to seek high-quality M&As when their wealth is insensitive to the acquirer’s performance (Agrawal and Mandelker 1987). We employ the fraction of CEOs’ pay that is not fixed salary as a proxy for the variable incentive portion of CEOs’ compensation followingEllahie et al. (2017). We find that CEO variable pay is negatively associated with IRI, although the coefficient is insignificant at conventional levels (column (2) inTable 5). This result suggests that misaligned incentives between managers and shareholders might be a driver of high IRI. 5.1.2. Financial Discipline of the Acquirer. Next, to explore whether disciplined acquirers are less likely to overestimate synergies and overpay, we examine the relation between IRI and the financial discipline of the acquirer using two proxies: an indicator for banks and insurance acquirers and the acquirer’s effort in due diligence. Return on equity is a key financial metric for banks and insurance companies given its strong link to prudential capital requirements. Because prudential capital buffers are negatively affected by unanticipated shocks to earnings and assets—from, for example, an ill-conceived acquisition—acquiring managers at banks and insurance companies should exercise greater financial discipline. Consistent with this conjecture, column (3) inTable 5shows that an indicator variable for bank and insurance acquirers is negatively associated with IRI. As a proxy for acquirers’ due diligence effort, we also examine the length of time from deal announcement to completion followingAmel-Zadeh and Zhang (2015),Marquardt and Zur (2015), andWangerin (2019). We expect higher due diligence effort to indicate greater financial discipline on the part of the acquirer, which in turn, should reduce the IRI component related to overestimating synergies or overpaying. Indeed, we observe that IRI is negatively associated with the number of days from M&A announcement to completion (column (4) inTable 5). This association becomes statistically insignificant when the regression includes all the determinants of IRI considered inSection 5.1(column (7) inTable 5). Viewed together, these findings suggest that financial discipline reduces the likelihood that IRI captures overestimation of synergies or overpayment and increases the likelihood that IRI captures attainable expected synergies.19 5.1.3. Difficulty in Estimating M&A Synergies. IRI is more likely to capture overestimated synergies or overpayment when estimating synergies is more difficult because of greater information uncertainty about the target. We thus construct two proxies for pre-M&A information uncertainty: (1)Target’s forecast dispersionmeasured as the standard deviation of the one-year-ahead EPS forecasts in Institutional Brokers’ Estimate System (I/B/E/S) in the most recent month prior to the M&A announcement (e.g.,Zhang 2006a) and (2)Target’s return volatilitymeasured as the standard deviation of monthly stock returns over the three years prior to the M&A (Zhang 2006b,Ellahie and Peng 2021). We find that both measures are positively and statistically significantly associated with IRI (columns (5) and (6) inTable 5); the associations remain statistically significant when the regression includes all the determinants of IRI considered inSection 5.1(column (7) inTable 5). On balance, these results suggest that greater information uncertainty about the target increases the component of IRI related to synergy overestimation. To summarize,Table 5shows that acquirer CEO overconfidence and misaligned incentives, the financial discipline of the acquirer, and the difficulty in estimating synergies are potential drivers of IRI. These results are in line with the conceptual framework developed inSection 2.4. 5.2. Explaining the Negative Relation Between IRI and Acquirer Performance In this subsection, we study specific channels through which IRI negatively predicts future ROE growth. We find that, on average, IRI predicts higher post-M&A operating costs (related to overestimated synergies), reduced liquidity, harsher financial constraints, and lower investment (related to overpayment) as well as more goodwill impairment (related to overpayment). 5.2.1. Underestimated Costs as a Channel. To examine whether IRI predicts the acquirer’s post-M&A operating cost structure, we regress change in the ratio of costs of goods sold to sales, operating costs including depreciation and amortization (D&A) to sales, operating costs excluding D&A to sales, and restructuring costs to sales in each of the first three years after the M&A on IRI and the control variables. The regression is the same as model (6) except that the dependent variable has become change in the relevant cost ratio (i.e., cost scaled by sales); scaling by sales accounts for volume changes and any effects of equity financing on the results. The results reveal that IRI is insignificantly associated with post-M&A change in the ratio of the acquirer’s cost of goods sold (COGS) to sales (column (1) inTable 6, panel A). In other words, the results fail to reject the null hypothesis that changes in product/service costs (i.e., direct costs and allocated overheads) vary with IRI. We proxy for restructuring costs using the Compustat variablespi(special items), which captures unusual or infrequent items reported as a separate component of income from continuing operations. As such, we expect at least some of the unforeseen costs related to the M&A, such as restructuring charges, to be included in this variable (Bens and Johnston 2009,Lee 2014). We multiply this variable by −1 to interpret it as an expense variable consistent with the rest of the cost analysis. In short, IRI appears to reflect underestimated costs associated with the M&A (i.e., overestimated synergies). To assess whether IRI captures overestimated synergies, we compute the merged firm’s combined forecasted net income (NI) for the three years after the M&A by summing the pre-M&A net income forecasts in I/B/E/S for the stand-alone acquirer and the stand-alone target. We then compute the difference between the acquirer’s actual net income after the M&A and the combined forecasted net income and scale this difference by the acquirer’s sales. This measure is positive (negative) if the acquirer has underestimated (overestimated) the acquisition’s synergies. Column (5) inTable 6, panel A shows that IRI is negatively associated with this measure of underestimated synergies, which further corroborates the results concerning changes in cost ratios. These results are consistent with IRI capturing overestimated synergies. 5.2.2. Reduced Liquidity and Foregone Investment as a Channel. After overpaying for a target, an acquirer may later face liquidity issues and be forced to forego valuable investment opportunities. To test this conjecture, we replace the dependent variable of model (6) with the change in liquidity (measured by the ratio of cash holdings to sales), the change in financial constraints (measured by theKaplan and Zingales 1997(KZ) Index, and theWhited and Wu 2006(WW) Index), and the change in investment (measured by the ratio of capital expenditures to net property, plant, and equipment) in the first three years after the M&A. The regression results inTable 6, panel B reveal that changes in cash and capital expenditures are negatively associated with IRI, whereas the KZ Index and the WW Index are positively associated with IRI. The results are similar when replacing IRI with the high-IRI indicator in the regressions (Table A.9, panel B in the Online Appendix). These results suggest that high-IRI acquirers experience lower liquidity, more stringent financial constraints, and lower investment after the M&A. 5.2.3. Goodwill Impairment as a Channel. Acquirers must write off goodwill created in M&As when the fair value of the goodwill declines: that is, when the acquirer realizes it overpaid for the target’s net assets. We thus examine whether IRI is associated with subsequent goodwill impairments. As in our analysis of changes in ROE, we focus on goodwill impairments—the ratio of pretax goodwill impairments (Compustat itemgdwlip) to prior period total assets—in the first three years post-M&A. The average goodwill impairments are 0.7%, 3.4%, and 3.3% of total assets in the first, second, and third years, respectively. Goodwill impairments concentrate among acquirers with high IRIs, as depicted in panel (a) of Figure A.2 in the Online Appendix. Whereas the average goodwill impairment is close to zero in all three years for low-IRI acquirers, it soars to about 10% in the second and third years after high-IRI M&As. Similarly, panel (b) of Figure A.2 in the Online Appendix shows that the mean likelihood of goodwill impairment in all three years is greater after high-IRI M&As than after low-IRI M&As. Next, we estimate model (6) with the dependent variable replaced with the following three measures of goodwill impairment: goodwill impairments as a percentage of assets, an indicator of goodwill impairments, and an indicator of large goodwill impairments that are at least 20% of total assets. We additionally control for a sequential deal indicator because goodwill impairments may be related to previous M&As undertaken by the same acquirer. We also control for acquirer goodwill in the deal completion year because goodwill impairments are more likely when the acquirer has more goodwill on its books.Table 6, panel C presents the regression results. The coefficients on IRI are positive and statistically significant at the 5% level throughout the three columns. The inference remains qualitatively unchanged when we replace IRI with the high-IRI indicator in Table A.9, panel C in the Online Appendix. The results are not driven by goodwill impairments for previous M&As undertaken by the acquirer; the coefficient on the sequential acquisition indicator is insignificant.20In short, the results are consistent with the notion that overpayment is a channel through which IRI negatively predicts ROE growth. Viewed together, the results suggest that high-IRI acquirers perform worse because of post-M&A operational and financial difficulties. Acquirers with overconfident, weakly incentivized, and poorly disciplined managers have higher IRIs, and IRI increases as it becomes more difficult to estimate synergies. These findings are consistent with the conceptual framework discussed inSection 2.4, which postulates that a high IRI could capture overestimated synergies or overpayment.
article.6. IRI and Post-Acquisition Security Ret: So far, we have focused on IRI’s predictive power for post-M&A performance using accounting-based outcomes, such as ROE, cost ratios, liquidity, investments, and goodwill impairments. This section shows that IRI also negatively predicts post-M&A returns to the acquirer’s shareholders and debtholders. The results are useful for public investors who can estimate the IRI once the M&A is publicly announced. 6.1. IRI and Post-Acquisition Returns to Acquirers’ Shareholders Prior studies have used acquirer-target combined announcement returns as a proxy for M&A quality based on the premise that stock market responses reflect deal quality. Because results indicate that IRI is an inverse measure of M&A quality, we expect IRI to be negatively associated with combined announcement returns. We compute three-day (days −1 to +1 around deal announcement) CARs to the acquirer, the target, and the acquirer and the target combined (weighted by their market capitalization on the second day before deal announcement). We then regress the combined announcement CARs on IRI (or the high-IRI indicator) and the deal characteristics associated with announcement returns (Betton et al. 2008). The regression results, presented in columns (1) and (2) in Table A.10 in the Online Appendix, show that the coefficient on IRI is negative and statistically insignificant, whereas the coefficient on the high-IRI indicator is negative and statistically significant at the 5% level. We replace the dependent variable with the acquirer’s three-day CARs in columns (3) and (4) in Table A.10 in the Online Appendix and with the target’s three-day CARs in columns (5) and (6) in Table A.10 in the Online Appendix. The regression results reveal that IRI is negatively (positively) associated with the acquirer’s (the target’s) announcement returns, but the associations are largely statistically insignificant. These results suggest that the information content of an IRI is not fully incorporated into stock returns around M&A announcement. We thus expect IRI to predict worse long-run stock returns to the acquirer and test this conjecture using the following regression: For brevity, we only present the regression results for monthly stock returns over the first 12 months in columns (1) and (2) inTable 7, panel A.21In column (1) inTable 7, panel A, the coefficient on IRI is negative but statistically insignificant; in column (2) inTable 7, panel A, the coefficient on the high-IRI indicator is negative and statistically significant at the 1% level. In terms of the economic magnitude, high-IRI acquirers underperform low-IRI acquirers by about 1.6% per month or 19% annually. article.7. Conclusions: We develop and quantify a measure of M&A deal quality based on established accounting and finance principles. This measure, IRI, is the minimum amount of additional return on equity the acquirer must generate from the target firm to justify the acquisition price. IRI is conceptually analogous to IRR; an investment project adds value to the firm when IRR is greater than the cost of capital. Similarly, an M&A benefits the acquirer if IRI is smaller than the attainable improvement in the target’s ROE post-M&A. A greater IRI is more challenging for the acquirer to deliver, ceteris paribus. We thus propose that IRI is an inverse measure of M&A quality and conjecture that high-IRI acquirers have worse post-M&A performance. Consistent with this conjecture, we find that high-IRI acquisitions are associated with lower post-M&A accounting returns and lower returns to shareholders and debtholders. IRI’s power to predict acquirer performance is incremental to the inputs for the computation of IRI, deal characteristics, and a host of variables proposed in prior studies to explain the acquirer’s post-M&A performance. Our findings suggest that managers who undertake high-IRI M&As, on average, cannot deliver the improvement over the target’s operating performance implied by the price paid for the target. High IRI appears to capture overpayment or overestimated synergies. In tests of the economic mechanisms, we find that acquirers with overconfident and weakly incentivized CEOs have higher IRIs, whereas more financially disciplined acquirers have lower IRIs. IRI also increases as estimating synergies becomes more difficult because of greater information uncertainty about the target. These economic mechanisms may explain why IRI reflects overestimated synergies or overpayment. In addition, operational and financial difficulties lead to acquirers’ worsening performance after high-IRI M&As. These acquirers incur greater operating costs, some of which are unforeseen. High-IRI acquirers also experience lower liquidity, more stringent financial constraints, foregone investments, and larger and more frequent goodwill impairments. IRI’s advantages lie in its solid theoretical foundation, computational ease, ex ante nature, and robust predictive power for acquirers’ post-M&A financial performance. Because M&A deal quality has multiple dimensions, IRI complements alternative M&A quality measures that are based on other dimensions and have their own advantages. For example, corporate governance-based measures (discussed inSection 4.2) can help potential acquirers improve governance and avoid value-destructive M&As. Measures based on similarities and overlaps between acquirers’ and targets’ products, technology, human capital, and bank network focus on the source of potential synergies. These measures enhance our understanding of how these synergies are created rather than on quantifying them. IRI also complements alternative financial measures of M&As, such as acquirer-target combined stock returns around M&A announcements and the widely used EPS accretion or dilution. These advantages, we maintain, make IRI a reliable and useful measure, particularly for practitioners attempting to assess the financial quality of M&A deals. Acquirer managers can compute the IRI for a hypothetical target firm at a certain bid premium both before approaching the target firm and during the negotiations. In this sense, IRI is a useful ex ante measure for gauging the quality of an M&A before the acquirer undertakes it. Investors can also compute IRI for an M&A if the acquirer and the target are public firms and once the M&A deal is announced, use the IRI estimate to guide their investment decisions. Of course, there are limitations to IRI’s applicability. For one, although IRI has broad applicability across different strategic rationales for M&A, it may be less useful for evaluating deals where financial synergies are less important. Additionally, although IRI inputs are readily available for public acquirers and targets, investors will inevitably struggle to estimate an IRI when the acquirer and the target are both private firms. Nonetheless, we believe that these limitations do not undercut the value of IRI as a measure of deal quality for M&As with available inputs. Furthermore, when M&As involve private firms, IRI can still usefully guide acquiring managers and advisors who often have access to confidential information shared by the target during the M&A process. Overall, we conclude that IRI is a reliable measure of M&A quality that is useful for academics and practitioners.
